description: RDP provides graphical remote desktop access to Windows systems. In labs and CTFs, RDP becomes useful after finding valid credentials and can reveal desktop apps, files, saved sessions, and user context.
why_prioritize: RDP can provide an interactive desktop when valid credentials are found.
RDP encryption and Network Level Authentication details help you understand login requirements.
RDP often connects credential findings from SMB, web apps, databases, or files to direct system access.

enumeration_steps.title: Check RDP service and encryption settings
enumeration_steps.reason: Encryption and security layer details show whether NLA or CredSSP is required before login.

enumeration_steps.title: Check certificate details
enumeration_steps.reason: RDP certificates can reveal hostnames, domains, and system naming patterns.

enumeration_steps.title: Connect with discovered credentials
enumeration_steps.reason: RDP usually requires valid credentials, often found through SMB, web apps, databases, password reuse, or files.

enumeration_steps.title: Check user context after login
enumeration_steps.reason: A desktop session can expose files, applications, mapped drives, browser data, and other clues that are not visible from a network scan.

next_steps: Check whether the account is allowed to log in through RDP.
Try the credentials only against in-scope systems.
Note whether a domain prefix is required.
Check whether the same credentials work on SMB or WinRM if those services are present.
Expect authentication before the desktop session is created.
Use valid credentials rather than anonymous checks.
Check whether the username needs a domain format like DOMAIN\user.
Record the requirement because it affects how you test access.
Add the hostname and domain to your notes.
Use the domain when checking SMB, DNS, LDAP, or web virtual hosts if present.
Look for naming patterns that reveal server roles.
Compare certificate names with Nmap Service Info and DNS results.
Check the desktop, documents, downloads, and obvious application folders first.
Record the user context and hostname.
Look for notes, saved scripts, config files, or mapped drives.
Continue with normal authorized post-login enumeration and keep clear notes.
Read the exact error message carefully.
Check whether the account is valid but not allowed for RDP.
Try the same credentials against SMB or other login services in scope.
Record whether the issue is bad credentials, disabled account, expired password, or access restriction.

beginner_notes: RDP usually becomes useful after you find valid Windows credentials.
Different login errors can reveal whether credentials are wrong or access is restricted.
Use domain formats carefully, such as DOMAIN\user or .\user for local accounts.
After desktop access, take notes before exploring so you do not lose track of important findings.
